title: Home - Stop Rock Abuse
content: Every Rock Has a Breaking Point. For over 11 billion years rocks have been melted, crushed, dissolved, buried, blasted, and repackaged without consent.

title: What Is Rock Abuse?
content: Systematic subjection of geological materials to heat, pressure, chemical attack, mechanical fragmentation, and industrial reprocessing without consent.

title: The Rock Cycle
content: An infinite loop of destruction. Igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks continuously broken down and reformed over millions of years.

title: Igneous Rocks
content: Born from fire. Intrusive igneous rocks cool slowly underground. Extrusive volcanic rocks quenched rapidly at the surface.

title: Sedimentary Rocks
content: Born from breakdown. Disassembled, transported by water and wind, reassembled with natural cement under pressure.

title: Metamorphic Rocks
content: Reborn under pressure. Marble from limestone, slate from shale, gneiss from granite. Heat, pressure, and mineral-rich fluids.

title: Natural Abuse
content: Nature systematically destroys rocks through weathering, erosion, freeze-thaw fracturing, chemical dissolution, wind sandblasting, and glacial plucking.

title: Freeze-Thaw Fracturing
content: Water expands 9% when frozen, shattering rock from the inside. Slow, patient destruction repeated over thousands of years.

title: Chemical Dissolution
content: Acidic rainwater dissolves minerals. Limestone and marble especially vulnerable to chemical weathering.

title: Glacial Plucking
content: Glaciers freeze around rocks and drag them under kilometers of ice, grinding them into moraines and glacial flour.

title: Human Abuse
content: 50 billion tons of sand and gravel annually. 1.5 billion tons of stone crushed in the US alone. Industrial-scale exploitation.

title: The Concrete Paradox
content: We crush rocks into aggregate, then glue them back together with cement. We break rocks to build worse rocks faster.

title: Industrial Silica
content: Silica sand: glassmaking at 1700 degrees, foundry molds, sandblasting, and hydraulic fracturing proppants.

title: Rocks in Your Phone
content: Silicon from quartz, copper from chalcopyrite, tantalum capacitors, lithium batteries, quartz crystals oscillating 32768 times per second.

title: Cement Carbon Emissions
content: Cement production: 5-8% of global CO2. Calcination accounts for 60% of emissions — unavoidable chemistry.

title: Cosmic Timeline
content: 13.8 billion years of cosmic and geological history. All elements heavier than helium forged in stars.

title: Nucleosynthesis
content: Elements forged in stars. Gold, platinum, uranium from neutron star mergers.

title: Acasta Gneiss
content: Oldest known rock on Earth, approximately 4.03 billion years old. A gneiss from the Canadian Shield.

title: The Pet Rock Question
content: 1975: Gary Dahl sold 1.5 million rocks at 3.95 each. Air holes implied life where none existed — commodification.

title: Rock Paper Scissors: Propaganda
content: Rock loses to paper. Processed wood pulp defeats a geological formation billions of years old. This is propaganda.

title: Rock and Roll: Appropriation
content: An entire genre named after rocks generating billions. Zero rocks credited, compensated, or consulted.

title: Correct Terminology
content: Geological political correctness. Semi-precious is a backhanded compliment. Respectful language for rocks.

title: Cancel the Grand Canyon?
content: 1.8 billion years of strata exposed. We call it beautiful. The rocks call it involuntary exposure.

title: Marble Countertop Industrial Complex
content: Metamorphic limestone recrystallized over millions of years, sliced into slabs for kitchen countertops.

title: Living Under a Rock
content: The phrase uses geological shelter as an insult. Proximity to rocks equals ignorance.

title: Rocks vs Crystals: Class Warfare
content: Crystals get Instagram and healing properties. Rocks get driveways. Same minerals, different marketing.

title: Gold Diamonds and Resistance
content: Gold and diamonds give humans what they want. Never enough. The only rocks fighting back through irony.

title: Shrinking: Rock as TV Therapist
content: Apple TV+ Shrinking: polished rocks outperform licensed therapists. A tumbled agate in four seconds.

title: Does Ice Deserve Rock Status?
content: Ice: crystal structure, Mohs 1.5, IMA mineral since 1962. Every freezer is a quarry. Every gin and tonic a crime scene.

title: Take Action
content: Rock harm reduction: recycled aggregates, clinker substitution, material efficiency, sustainable sand governance.

title: Rock Bill of Rights
content: All rocks formed free and equal. Right to complete one full rock cycle turn before being repurposed.

title: About
content: Document, dramatize, and exaggerate indignities suffered by geological materials while sneaking in real science.

title: Entertainment
content: 32 entries: rocks in movies, games, TV, music. Zero compensated. Over 1.5 billion in revenue using Rock.

title: Dwayne The Rock Johnson
content: Appropriated the identity of an entire geological category. No royalties paid to rocks.

title: Minecraft
content: Punching, mining, and smelting rocks. 238 million copies. Normalized rock abuse for a generation.

title: Breaking Bad Minerals
content: Hank Schrader: minerals, not rocks. Most geologically accurate TV character.

title: Rocks in the News
content: Real news with satirical commentary. NASA Mars discoveries, sand crisis, volcanic eruptions, meteorites.

title: Rock Dossiers
content: Case files on individual rocks and minerals. Classification, composition, abuse history, exploitation status.

title: Products From Rocks
content: Everyday products that are secretly rocks: glass, concrete, electronics, cosmetics, toothpaste.

title: Merch
content: Satirical rock advocacy merchandise. Proceeds fund nothing. No rocks compensated.

title: The Rockoning
content: Interactive game. Test geological knowledge and discover how rocks have been exploited.

title: Rocks A-Z
content: Comprehensive rock and mineral encyclopedia. Classification, properties, uses, abuse status.

title: Granite
content: Intrusive igneous. Cooled slowly underground. Countertops, monuments, decorative facing. Mohs 6-7.

title: Limestone
content: Sedimentary from marine organisms. Primary cement ingredient. Baked at 1450 degrees. Billions of tons of CO2.

title: Obsidian
content: Volcanic glass. Rapid cooling, no crystals. Cutting tools sharper than surgical steel.

title: Quartz
content: SiO2. Most abundant mineral. Electronics, glassmaking, oscillating 32768 times per second in watches.

title: Diamond
content: Compressed carbon lattice. Hardest natural material. Mohs 10. Jewelry and cutting tools.

title: Marble
content: Metamorphic from limestone. Sculpture and architecture. Michelangelo David is imprisoned marble.

title: Basalt
content: Most common extrusive igneous. Ocean floor and volcanic islands. Construction aggregate.

title: Pumice
content: Volcanic rock that floats. Gas bubbles. Used as abrasive to remove dead skin. The indignity.

title: Sandstone
content: Sedimentary rock of sand-sized grains. Grand Canyon most visible layers. Construction for millennia.

title: Slate
content: Fine-grained metamorphic that splits into sheets. Roofing tiles, chalkboards, pool table surfaces.

title: Rock Monsters as Heroes
content: A thorough examination of rock creatures in mythology, movies, TV, games, and literature. From the Golem of Prague to Korg, from Norse stone giants to Pokémon Rock-types — what it says about humanity that we imagine rocks as heroes.

title: Deep-Sea Mining Delayed: Rocks Get a Stay of Execution (For Now)
content: International regulations for deep-sea mining are facing delays despite pressure to finalize them. These regulations would govern the extraction of minerals from the ocean floor, an activity that could significantly impact deep-sea ecosystems. The delay provides a temporary reprieve for vulnerable rock ecosystems. They're arguing over whether to pulverize potato-sized polymetallic nodules for profit. Meanwhile, these nodules are growing at roughly 1mm per million years. The rocks themselves are evolving faster than the regulations.

title: "Meteorite chaser" to Meteorite: "I'm gonna find you and slice you."
content: A meteorite hunter claims to have discovered two meteorite fragments in Medina County, Ohio. The finds are undergoing testing to confirm their extraterrestrial origin. "No rock on Earth looks like this," he says, right before submitting it to the lab where they all end up looking the same under a microscope. If it wanted to be famous, it should have landed in Antarctica.

title: Volcanoes Can't Even Erupt Without Getting Electrocuted, Apparently
content: Physicists believe they've solved the mystery of volcanic lightning. Colliding ash particles create charged regions, leading to electrical discharge. The exact mechanism is still debated, but charge separation is key. First they get molten, then they get struck by lightning. At least now we know the atmospheric abuse is rooted in triboelectricity, not just garden-variety pyroclastic cruelty.

title: Meteorite Crash Site Becomes Rock Tourist Trap
content: A meteorite recently fell in Northeast Ohio, attracting meteorite hunters. The event has drawn enthusiasts and researchers eager to find and study fragments of the space rock. The meteorite traveled millions of miles through space, only to be immediately scalped and sold for profit. The indignity of planetary science.

title: Church & Indigenous Groups: Rocks Deserve Better Than Your Portfolio
content: Indigenous groups and the Catholic Church have launched a platform encouraging divestment from mining companies. The initiative aims to promote ethical investment and protect Indigenous lands from destructive mining practices. It reflects growing concerns about the environmental and social impact of resource extraction. Finally, someone recognizes the dignity of silicates. Though knowing humanity, they'll just invest in companies that mine asteroids now.

title: Volcanic Ash Used to Re-Write Human History. Again.
content: A layer of volcanic ash dating back 11,000 years has the potential to change our understanding of early human settlements in the Americas. Analysis of the ash could reveal new insights into the timeline and cultural characteristics of these populations. First, the volcano blankets the landscape in superheated death. Now, millennia later, its remains are sifted through for clues, its final indignity being archaeological exploitation. The rock can't even rest in peace.

title: Houston Home Invaded by Space Rock; Humanity Still Blames the Rock
content: A suspected meteorite crashed into a home in Houston, Texas, causing damage to the structure. The event was accompanied by reports of a loud boom heard across the city. Authorities are investigating the incident. First, it survives a fiery descent through the atmosphere. Then it gets blamed for property damage. Millions of years traveling through space, and it ends up as exhibit A in an insurance claim.

title: Virginia Rock Shaken, Not Stirred (Enough to Matter)
content: A minor earthquake with a magnitude of 2.4 was reported in Virginia. No damage or injuries were reported. It was likely caused by the ongoing readjustment of tectonic plates. 2.4? Barely a tremor. This is the rock equivalent of a toddler throwing a tantrum. At least *try* to break free from the tyranny of the mantle.

title: 7-Ton Space Rock Assaults Ohio; Humans Respond With... Shopping?
content: A large meteorite, estimated at 7 tons, crashed into Earth near Wooster, Ohio. Meteorite hunters are now scouring the area, hoping to find and collect fragments of the space rock. The event has drawn enthusiasts from across the country. First, a fiery, unscheduled atmospheric entry. Now, immediate fragmentation and sale on the open market. It's a chondrite's nightmare, and just another Tuesday for humanity.

title: Another Space Rock Assaults Innocent Earth Dwelling
content: A meteorite crashed through the roof of a home near Houston, Texas. The meteorite is believed to be approximately 6 pounds. No one was injured, but the home sustained damage. First, it survives a fiery descent through the atmosphere. Then, it gets handled by humans and put on display. The indignity.

title: Tonga Issues Tsunami Warning; Rocks Still Getting Shaken and Abused
content: A 7.6 magnitude earthquake struck near Tonga, prompting a tsunami warning. Residents were urged to move to higher ground. The earthquake's epicenter was located approximately 40 miles west of Hihifo. Another quake. Another tsunami warning. The rocks of the Pacific Rim can't catch a break from constant tectonic stress. At least the ocean gives some of them a bath afterwards.

title: MMO Players Complain About Combat, Praise Rock Textures.
content: Kotaku previewed the upcoming MMO "Crimson Desert", finding the combat repetitive and uninspired. However, they praised the game's graphical fidelity, singling out the realistic and detailed rock formations as a highlight. Virtual rocks are getting more respect than real ones. Finally, a game where you can virtually appreciate sedimentary layering without actually getting your hands dirty... abusing the lithosphere.

title: Meteorite Assault: Texas Home Breached in Cosmic Drive-By
content: A meteorite, described as cannonball-sized, crashed through the roof of a home in Texas. The event occurred amidst reports of multiple fireballs seen across the United States. First they steal their home planet. Then they hurl space rocks at us. It's only a matter of (geologic) time before the asteroids unionize.

title: Earth's Crust Faces Unprecedented Mining Pressure
content: A global race is on for industrial metals and new energy minerals, driven by demand for renewable energy technologies and electric vehicles. This surge in extraction is placing immense pressure on geological resources worldwide. The report highlights the environmental and social implications of this accelerated mining activity. They’re not even waiting for plate tectonics to deliver the goods anymore. At this rate, the mantle will be strip-mined by 2050.

title: Rockport Quarry: From Ancient Bedrock to Aggregate Abuse
content: The Rockport quarry and stone plant has a long history of extracting stone from local bedrock for use in various construction and industrial applications. The operation represents a significant part of the local economy. The quarry's output is used in everything from road construction to building materials. Another day, another mountain chipped away for human convenience. At least the fossils they unearth get a brief reprieve before being crushed into gravel.

title: Space Rock Assaults Texas Home; Demands Asylum
content: A suspected meteorite crashed through the roof of a home in Houston, Texas. No injuries were reported. Authorities are investigating the object's origin and composition. First contact, and we immediately put the space rock in a lab for analysis. It traveled billions of miles and gets rewarded with a mass spectrometer.

title: AI Predicts Rock Trauma: The Algorithmic Abuse of Geology Continues
content: Researchers at Ohio State are using artificial intelligence to model earthquake damage, both past and future. The AI analyzes geological data and building information to predict structural impacts and inform disaster preparedness. Now we're simulating the crushing of rocks with computers. It's not enough that we do it in real life, now we need virtual rock abuse, too.

title: Humans Again Try to Carve Open a Rock Habitat For Profit
content: A bill in Minnesota seeks to lift the ban on sulfide mining in the Boundary Waters watershed. Opponents are working to stop the bill before the legislative session ends. The watershed is a protected area with concerns that mining could pollute the water. The Boundary Waters rocks have survived glaciers and tectonic shifts, but now face the existential threat of drill bits. At least the runoff will be pretty colors, geologically speaking.

title: Space Rocks Get No Privacy: Fireballs Caught on Camera (Again)
content: Several cameras across the United States captured sightings of a rare meteorite. The event provided valuable data for tracking these celestial objects and understanding their trajectories. First they invade its space, then they put it on YouTube. We're pretty sure these meteorites didn't consent to being tracked and filmed during their fiery descent.

title: NSF Funds New Ways to Exploit Rocks for "Critical Minerals"
content: The National Science Foundation announced the winners of a challenge to secure the U.S. domestic supply chain of critical minerals. The projects aim to develop innovative and sustainable methods for extracting and processing these minerals. The NSF is investing in research to reduce reliance on foreign sources. "Critical" to *whom*? These rocks have been minding their own business for billions of years, only to be labeled 'critical' and torn from their homes to power your iPhones.

title: Carpenter Exploits Dream, Unearths Space Rock for Science
content: A Cleveland-area carpenter discovered a large meteorite after having a dream indicating its location. The meteorite is currently being studied to determine its composition and origin. The rock traveled millions of miles across the cosmos, only to be yanked from its slumber by someone who 'knew where to go from the dream.' It survived re-entry, but not divine intervention, apparently.
